learning_objectives: Articulate why mobile-first design leads to better products across all platforms
Identify constraints unique to mobile: screen size, attention, context, connectivity
Apply content prioritization strategies for small screens
readings: Mobile First by Luke Wroblewski, Ch. 1-3
Touch Design for Mobile Interfaces by Steven Hoober, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile is not a smaller desktop — it is a fundamentally different context of use. Designing mobile-first forces prioritization and focus that improves the experience on every platform.
key_concepts: Mobile-first as a design constraint that forces content prioritization
Mobile context: one-handed use, distraction, variable connectivity, location awareness
Thumb zones: natural reach areas for one-handed smartphone use
Performance budgets: every kilobyte matters on mobile networks
Progressive enhancement: build for the most constrained platform first
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three apps that succeed on mobile by embracing constraints — identifying specific design decisions driven by mobile context

review.value: Research by Steven Hoober shows most users hold phones in one hand — the bottom center of the screen is the easiest to reach, the top corners are hardest. Place primary actions in the easy-reach zone.
Start with a core experience that works on the most constrained device, then add features and richness for more capable devices — ensures universal usability

learning_objectives: Compare iOS Human Interface Guidelines with Material Design principles
Design platform-appropriate interfaces that respect user expectations
Decide when to follow platform conventions vs. creating custom patterns
readings: Apple Human Interface Guidelines: iOS Design Themes
Material Design 3: Design foundations and components

lecture_notes.introduction: iOS and Android users have different expectations about navigation, gestures, and visual patterns. Respecting platform conventions reduces learning curve and increases user confidence.
key_concepts: iOS conventions: tab bar navigation, back swipe gesture, sheets, SF Symbols
Android conventions: bottom navigation, FAB, top app bar, Material icons
Platform-specific patterns: iOS action sheets vs. Android bottom sheets
Cross-platform decisions: when to unify and when to diverge
Platform typography: SF Pro (iOS) vs. Roboto (Android)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the same feature — a photo sharing flow — for both iOS and Android, highlighting where designs diverge to respect platform conventions
setup_requirements: Figma with iOS and Android component libraries
iOS and Android devices for reference

review.value: iOS: tab bar at bottom with 3-5 items, back via left-edge swipe or back button. Android: bottom navigation or navigation drawer, back via system back button/gesture.
Follow platform conventions for system-level patterns (navigation, settings, permissions). Custom patterns are acceptable for core product differentiators — but always user-test custom patterns.

learning_objectives: Design mobile navigation systems that scale from 3 to 50+ destinations
Implement tab bar, drawer, hub-and-spoke, and embedded navigation patterns
Handle deep linking and state restoration in mobile apps
readings: Designing Web Navigation by James Kalbach, Ch. 8 (Mobile Navigation)

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile navigation must balance access to content with screen real estate. The right navigation pattern depends on the breadth and depth of your content architecture.
key_concepts: Tab bar: best for 3-5 equal-priority destinations
Navigation drawer: for apps with 5+ sections of varying importance
Hub-and-spoke: for task-focused apps with independent workflows
Search-first: when content volume makes browsing impractical
Deep linking: preserving state and enabling external entry points
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing navigation for a health and fitness app with 8 sections of varying priority — evaluating tab bar, drawer, and hybrid approaches
setup_requirements: IA sitemap for the demo app

review.value: Maximum 5 items; more creates cramped, unreadable icons. If you have more than 5 top-level sections, combine some, use a 'More' tab, or switch to a navigation drawer.

URLs that open a specific screen within an app — essential for sharing, notifications, and marketing. Every screen should have a deep link, and the app must handle arriving at any screen without prior context.

learning_objectives: Design for variable network conditions: fast, slow, and offline
Implement skeleton screens and optimistic UI patterns
Create offline-capable experiences with clear connectivity status
readings: Designing for Performance by Lara Hogan, Ch. 1-3
Offline First: Mobile web design patterns (A List Apart)

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile users experience variable connectivity constantly — in elevators, on subways, in rural areas. Designing for degraded and offline states is not an edge case; it is a core requirement.
key_concepts: Skeleton screens: showing layout structure before content loads
Optimistic UI: showing the expected result immediately, syncing in background
Offline-first architecture: cache content for offline access
Connectivity status indicators: when and how to show network state
Image optimization: responsive images, lazy loading, progressive JPEG
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the loading and offline states for a messaging app — skeleton screens, cached messages, queued outgoing messages, and connectivity restoration
setup_requirements: Chrome DevTools for network throttling simulation

review.value: Gray placeholder shapes matching the layout of content that is loading — feels faster than a spinner because it gives users a preview of the page structure and reduces perceived wait time
Show the expected outcome immediately (message sent, item added to cart) and sync with the server in the background — if it fails, show an error and offer retry. Makes the app feel instant.

learning_objectives: Design onboarding flows that maximize activation without overwhelming users
Time permission requests for maximum grant rates
Measure onboarding effectiveness with funnel analytics

lecture_notes.introduction: The first 60 seconds of a mobile app determine whether users stay or uninstall. Onboarding must rapidly demonstrate value, build trust, and gather just enough information to personalize the experience.
key_concepts: Onboarding patterns: progressive, benefits-oriented, function-oriented
The first-run experience: what users see before they have any data
Permission priming: explaining value before the system dialog appears
Just-in-time permissions: requesting when the feature is needed, not at launch
Onboarding metrics: completion rate, time-to-value, day-1 retention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesigning the onboarding flow for a photo editing app — reducing steps, improving permission timing, and designing compelling empty states

review.value: Show a custom screen explaining why the permission is needed and the benefit of granting it BEFORE the system permission dialog — users who understand the value grant permissions at 2-3x higher rates
How long it takes a new user to experience the core value of the app — the number one metric for onboarding effectiveness. Every unnecessary onboarding step delays time-to-value.

questions.question: A designer creates a mobile app with the primary navigation at the top of the screen, a hamburger menu icon for section navigation, and all action buttons as small text links. Identify the mobile UX problems and propose fixes.
questions.answer: Problems: (1) Primary navigation at top is outside the easy thumb reach zone — should be at the bottom. (2) Hamburger menu hides navigation behind an extra tap, reducing discoverability — replace with a visible bottom tab bar for the top 4-5 sections. (3) Small text links for actions violate minimum touch target sizes (44x44pt iOS, 48x48dp Android) and are hard to tap accurately — replace with properly sized buttons with adequate spacing. Fixes: Move primary navigation to a bottom tab bar, make the most important action a prominent floating action button or primary button in the thumb zone, and size all interactive elements to minimum touch target standards.

questions.question: Explain the key differences between how you would design a settings screen for iOS versus Android. What should be platform-specific and what can remain the same?
questions.answer: Platform-specific differences: iOS settings use toggle switches (UISwitch), grouped table views with inset styling, and a back chevron for navigation. Android uses Material switches, preference categories with dividers, and relies on the system back gesture/button. iOS often integrates with the system Settings app for OS-level permissions, while Android handles permissions within the app. Can remain the same: the information architecture (grouping and order of settings), the labels and descriptions, and the overall content strategy. The settings content is universal — only the visual components and navigation patterns should respect platform conventions.

rubric.key: Identifies what should remain the same across platforms

questions.question: Compare skeleton screens with loading spinners. When is each appropriate, and what is the UX advantage of skeleton screens?
questions.answer: Skeleton screens are gray placeholder shapes matching the layout of the content that will appear. They are appropriate when the page structure is predictable and content loads within 1-3 seconds. UX advantages: (1) Users perceive faster loading because they see progress (layout appearing) rather than waiting (spinner spinning). (2) Users can orient to the page structure while content loads. (3) The transition from skeleton to content is smooth, not jarring. Loading spinners are appropriate when: (1) The content structure is unpredictable (search results may be a list or a no-results message). (2) The operation takes longer than 3 seconds (show a spinner with progress text). (3) A full-screen action is processing (payment, file upload).

rubric.key: Appropriate use cases for spinners identified

questions.question: An app requests camera, location, contacts, and notification permissions all during the first launch before the user has done anything. Redesign this permission strategy and explain your reasoning.
questions.answer: Redesigned strategy: (1) Remove all permission requests from first launch. Let users explore the app first. (2) Request camera permission just-in-time when the user taps 'Take Photo' for the first time, with a priming screen: 'To take photos, [App] needs access to your camera.' (3) Request location when the user opens the map or search-nearby feature, with priming: 'Allow location access to find results near you.' (4) Request contacts only if the user taps 'Invite Friends,' with explanation of how contacts will be used. (5) Request notifications after the user has completed their first key action and experienced value — 'Want to know when someone replies? Enable notifications.' Reasoning: just-in-time requests have 2-3x higher grant rates than upfront requests because users understand the value. Requesting everything upfront feels invasive and causes permission fatigue — users deny everything reflexively.

rubric.key: Moves all permissions to just-in-time context
Includes permission priming for each request
Delays notification permission until after value delivery
Explains higher grant rates as justification

major_assignments.title: Mobile App Design Project
major_assignments.description: Design a complete mobile app experience for both iOS and Android. Include onboarding, core navigation, key feature screens, offline states, and platform-specific adaptations. Deliver high-fidelity prototypes and test with mobile users.
objectives: Apply mobile-first design principles to a real app concept
Design platform-appropriate versions for both iOS and Android
Address mobile-specific concerns: performance, offline, permissions
Test prototypes on actual mobile devices with real users
deliverables: Mobile app concept document with target users and core value proposition
High-fidelity designs for iOS and Android (minimum 12 screens each)
Interactive prototype for primary user flow on both platforms
Design documentation showing platform-specific decisions and rationale
Usability test report from testing on actual mobile devices with 3+ users

criteria: Content prioritization reflects mobile constraints
Touch targets meet platform minimum sizes
Navigation is thumb-friendly and appropriate for content depth
Loading, empty, and error states designed for mobile context
iOS version follows Human Interface Guidelines conventions
Android version follows Material Design conventions
Platform-specific patterns used where appropriate
Shared elements maintain brand consistency across platforms
Prototype works on actual mobile devices
Interactions feel native and responsive
Key flow is complete and testable end-to-end
Transitions and animations are appropriate for mobile
Tested on real devices with real users
Findings document mobile-specific usability issues
Platform decision rationale is clear and well-reasoned
Iterations reflect testing insights
Thoughtful mobile-first design with proper constraint-driven prioritization
Respectful platform adaptation for both iOS and Android
Polished interactive prototype tested on real mobile devices
Well-documented platform decisions and user testing findings

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on mobile design principles, platform guidelines, and mobile UX patterns

platform_labs.description: Weekly labs designing and prototyping mobile patterns for iOS and Android

mobile_app_project.description: Complete mobile app design for both platforms with prototyping and testing

mobile_critique_journal.description: Weekly journal entries analyzing mobile apps for UX patterns and design decisions

final_presentation.description: Professional presentation of mobile app project to design critique panel

course_policies.attendance: Attendance expected for all sessions. Lab sessions include hands-on mobile prototyping that requires in-person participation. More than 3 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Mobile labs are due weekly for iterative feedback. Late labs penalized 10% per day. The final project has a firm deadline with 15% per day penalty.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion and peer critique encouraged. All design work and prototypes must be individual. Sharing Figma mobile component libraries is permitted; sharing designs is not.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs must be original. Tracing or copying screens from existing apps is prohibited. Use existing apps as inspiration and reference only, with proper attribution.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma with mobile device frames, at least one physical smartphone (iOS or Android) for prototype testing, and Figma Mirror app installed on the test device.
